prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the emergence of the first party system from 1787 to 1800.
student_response: The first political parties appeared soon after the Constitution, but the reasons are hard to rank because almost every dispute became political. Before 1787 the Articles of Confederation had left Congress unable to tax or regulate commerce. Shays' Rebellion frightened property owners, while other Americans feared that a new central government could become another British monarchy. Ratification therefore already left Federalists and Anti-Federalists suspicious of each other. Once the new government started operating, those old suspicions did not disappear. Hamilton wrote a financial program involving assumption of state debts, funding the national debt, and a national bank. Jefferson and Madison opposed much of it. The bank was especially disputed because Hamilton used a strict interpretation of the Constitution while Jefferson defended implied powers, which was really the reverse of their usual positions. Cabinet arguments then spread into newspapers such as the Gazette of the United States and the National Gazette. Foreign events made the arguments louder. The French Revolution divided Americans who admired republican France from those alarmed by its violence. Washington's Neutrality Proclamation and Jay's Treaty also caused controversy. Democratic-Republican societies criticized the administration, and Federalists thought the societies encouraged disorder like the Whiskey Rebellion. Later the XYZ Affair helped produce the Alien and Sedition Acts, and the Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions answered them. There were also personality problems. Hamilton distrusted Jefferson, Jefferson distrusted Hamilton, and Adams did not always agree with other Federalists. Elections rewarded people for organizing supporters, printing attacks, and identifying candidates with a side. By 1796 voters were choosing between groups even though the founders often said factions were dangerous. Party labels also appeared in congressional voting and local meetings. Candidates depended on letters, printers, tavern discussions, and allies in state legislatures because presidential electors were chosen differently from state to state. Not yet a modern campaign system. The election of 1800 then became especially bitter, with accusations going in every direction. All of these developments mattered. Economic plans, constitutional disagreements, newspapers, France, Britain, and ambitious leaders each played a role, so it is not really possible to identify a most important cause. The parties emerged because disagreements existed and people eventually took sides. In the end the Federalists generally favored order and the Republicans generally favored liberty, although both sides claimed both ideas. A political system that had been designed without parties somehow had parties by 1800.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing Hamiltonian and Jeffersonian visions for the United States from 1789 to 1800.
student_response: After independence, the weak Articles of Confederation could not tax well or manage trade, and arguments over the Constitution asked how powerful the new national government should be. Hamilton and Jefferson offered opposite answers: Hamilton wanted a commercial nation directed by an energetic federal government, while Jefferson prefered an agricultural republic protected by limited federal power, although both wanted the United States to remain independent of Europe. Hamilton's Report on Public Credit proposed that the federal government fund the debt and assume state debts. He also supported the Bank of the United States and a report encouraging manufactures. These items all belonged to his economic plan and were debated in Washington's cabinet. Hamilton usually read the necessary and proper clause broadly. Jefferson pictured independent yeoman farmers as the safest citizens. He was suspicious of cities, bankers, and a permanent debt, and he read federal powers more strictly. The two men also looked abroad differently. Jefferson sympathised with revolutionary France, while Hamilton trusted trade with Britian more. Jay's Treaty and Washington's neutrality became major controversies. There were still a few simularities. Neither man wanted the United States recolonized, both served in Washington's administration, and both thought their program would preserve republican government. Their supporters nevertheless formed rival newspapers and parties. Hamilton's followers became Federalists and Jefferson's became Democratic-Republicans. Thus their visions differed mainly over centralized finance and the kind of economy America should have, but each was presented as a way to save the same young republic.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing the effects of Revolutionary ideals on women and enslaved African Americans from 1776 to 1800.
student_response: The Revolution grew out of Enlightenment natural-right ideas and colonial resistance to a government in which Americans claimed they had no representation. Its language of equality affected people who had not possessed full political rights. From 1776 to 1800, Revolutionary ideals widened women's educational and civic role mainly through republican motherhood, while enslaved African Americans used the same ideals to challenge slavery and seek freedom; however, neither group gained equal national political citizenship. For women, the republic needed informed mothers who could raise virtuous republican sons. This idea encouraged more female education even though voting and officeholding remained male privileges. Abigail Adams's request that John Adams remember the ladies shows that some women pushed the language of Revolution further than male leaders intended. Women also participated in boycotts and managed households during the war. These actions made their contribution visible, but coverture continued to limit a married woman's independent legal identity. The Revolution did not end slavery either. Enslaved people petitioned state governments by using the Declaration's claim that all men were created equal. Thousands sought liberty by joining either British or American forces, depending on which side offered a route to freedom. Northern states such as Pennsylvania adopted gradual emancipation, while slavery remained central in the South. The Haitian Revolution also frightened slaveholders because it showed that revolutionary language could support an actual slave uprising. In 1848 women finally met at Seneca Falls and wrote the Declaration of Sentiments, which directly copied the Declaration of Independence. This proves that the Revolution immediately created a national women's rights movement. African Americans had a more direct struggle over literal freedom, whereas women's gains centered on education and their family role. Yet in both cases white male leaders accepted useful service more easily than equality, leaving the broad promise of the Revolution unfinished.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of conflict over trans-Appalachian expansion from 1763 to 1795.
student_response: The Proclamation Line of 1763. The Treaty of Fort Stanwix. Kentucky land claims. The Northwest Ordinance. Fallen Timbers and the Treaty of Greenville. These events all came from rival claims to the same western land. After the Seven Years' War, Britain gained a huge interior empire but also a large debt and the problem of preventing another expensive frontier war. Its attempted solution, the Proclamation of 1763, angered colonists already looking beyond the Appalachians. From 1763 to 1795, conflict over trans-Appalachian expansion was caused most importantly by settlers' and speculators' demand for land colliding with Indigenous nations' determination to preserve their homelands, while weak and changing imperial governments repeatedly made promises they could not enforce. Land hunger turned policy disputes into violence. Veterans expected western grants, wealthy men including George Washington invested in western claims, and poorer families crossed the mountains looking for farms. Britain's line did not stop them. Nor could treaties settle the issue when Native people who used the land had not all agreed to a sale. After independence, Virginia and other states also held overlapping claims until they ceded western territory to the Confederation. Indigenous confederacies were not simply reacting to a line on a map. Pontiac's resistance had helped convince Britain to restrict settlement in the first place. Later, Shawnee, Miami, and allied warriors in the Old Northwest defeated American armies, including St. Clair's force, while receiving some British support. Because the United States wanted revenue from western land and security for settlers, Washington's government sent another army. Anthony Wayne's victory at Fallen Timbers, followed by Greenville, opened much of Ohio to American settlement. Government weakness intensified the collisions. Britain announced a boundary but colonists ignored it. The Confederation Congress wrote the Northwest Ordinance and planned orderly territories, but possessed little military and financial power. The new federal government eventually used troops and treaties more effectively. Meanwhile Spain controlled the Mississippi outlet for western farmers until Pinckney's Treaty, making settlers wonder whether eastern officials valued them. So imperial rivalry and uncertain authority mattered, but mainly because they failed to resolve the underlying contest for land. Once American military power defeated the Native confederacy, expansion accelerated rather than the basic conflict disappearing.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining how evangelical religion contributed to antebellum reform from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: Finney's revivals, the burned-over district, temperance societies, abolitionists, and moral reform associations all reflected the same antebellum hope that people and society could be improved. The early republic had weakened some older established churches and expanded political democracy, while the market revolution uprooted families and created fast-growing towns, inequality, and anxiety about disorder. In that setting the Second Great Awakening made salvation emotional and open to ordinary choice rather than something fixed for a small elect. Evangelical religion contributed to reform from 1800 to 1848 by teaching that converted individuals could choose moral behavior and help perfect society, by giving women and other ordinary people organizations through which to act, and by creating national networks that connected local concern to large campaigns, although economic change and Enlightenment ideas also shaped reform. Charles Grandison Finney preached that listeners could decide to reject sin, he used the anxious bench and direct appeals at revivals, and the logic carried outside church because if a drunkard could choose sobriety or a sinner could choose salvation then reformers could attack the environments encouraging vice. Temperance became especially broad because alcohol was associated with domestic abuse, missed work, and poverty, and the American Temperance Society spread pledges and lectures across communities. The same perfectionist outlook encouraged immediate abolition. William Lloyd Garrison demanded an end to slavery on moral grounds in The Liberator, while the American Anti-Slavery Society used lecturers and petitions, evangelical converts such as Theodore Weld treated slaveholding as a national sin that demanded action now, not only a distant political problem. Religious organization mattered as much as doctrine, churches and voluntary societies supplied meeting spaces, printers, traveling speakers, and fundraising methods, and women who could not vote gained public experience by organizing fairs, petitions, and local chapters. This activism eventually produced conflict when the Grimke sisters spoke to mixed audiences and critics said public speaking violated women's proper sphere. The reform impulse also reached education, prisons, and care for people with disabilities. Horace Mann described common schools as a way to form disciplined republican citizens, while Dorothea Dix appealed to moral responsibility toward the mentally ill, even when their arguments were not narrowly evangelical. Religion was therefore a catalyst rather than the only cause, because urbanization made poverty and drinking more visible and middle-class employers valued punctual sober workers, while abolition also drew directly on Revolutionary natural rights. There were limits too, many churches split over slavery and some reform movements imposed Protestant middle-class standards on immigrants. Still, the connection between personal conversion and social perfection explains why so many different campaigns appeared together. Evangelicalism supplied both the moral urgency and the practical network, while contemporary economic disruption supplied many of the problems those reformers chose to attack.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing labor organization in northern factories and southern plantations from 1820 to 1848.
student_response: Labor in factories and on plantations was an important part of America because most things had to be produced by workers. In the North people went into factories and in the South people were on plantations, so both sections had labor even though the places looked different. The factory was usually in a large city and used assembly lines where each person made one tiny part of an automobile. They worked for the factory owner and the government often decided their pay. Some were immigrants and some were women. A plantation was in a rural place where crops grew, and its laborers lived close to the fields. A lot of them were sharecroppers who rented land after the cotton harvest and then paid the owner. They did not have much money either. Work days in both systems could be long. Bells told northern workers when to start, while southern labor was based on whether the crop needed attention. Because cotton was difficult to grow, plantation owners purchased machines and factories usually did everything by hand. Northern workers could sometimes leave, but most could not because the law tied them to a factory contract for life. Southern workers had somewhat more choice because they could move to a different plantation after paying their yearly debt. These facts show that work was work. Owners wanted products and workers wanted to survive. There were rich people and poor people in both regions, and this made arguments between sections. Factories produced cloth and plantations produced food, metal, and other goods. Then the goods were sold in markets. Labor organization also means workers forming organizations. Some factory workers would meet and plantation workers also communicated while working. These organizations later turned into the large unions that ran national politics. The North and South kept growing apart because their jobs were not the same, yet they were similar because neither kind was easy. There are many ways to compare them and also many ways they cannot be compared. It depends if labor means the job, the worker, or the organization. Overall labor was organized according to where people lived and what the owner wanted them to do, which changed all the time from 1820 to 1848.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in federal economic policy from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: From 1800 to 1848, federal economic policy changed from a stated preference for limited national action toward broader federal promotion of growth, but it continuously provoked disagreement over whether economic development belonged to the nation or to the states. The greatest change was the increasing expectation that public authority should help connect regions and encourage commerce, while the strongest continuity was suspicion that national economic power would favor one section or class. Economic policy affected banking, transportation, trade, public land, and government revenue. National leaders had to decide whether one policy could fit agricultural districts, commercial ports, and newer settlements. Some citizens wanted stable credit and coordinated development. Others believed that distant officials would make rules benefiting financiers and manufacturers. These positions did not remain attached permanently to one region or one political label. A policy could seem useful when it aided local producers and unconstitutional when its costs appeared elsewhere. Transportation illustrates the dispute in a general way. Better routes lowered shipping time and linked farmers to wider markets, so demands for public support increased as settlement moved farther inland. Yet arguments continued about who should pay, whether projects were local or national, and which communities would receive the gains. Similar disagreements surrounded banking. A broad financial institution could make payments more uniform and assist government finance, but opponents saw concentrated privilege and worried that credit decisions escaped popular control. Tariff policy raised another sectional argument because protection could assist domestic production while making imported goods more expensive and inviting retaliation against exports. These recurring controversies show why economic policy expanded unevenly. The federal government was not simply growing in a straight line, and it was not simply retreating. National officials sometimes accepted developmental aims but rejected a particular method. At other times, state governments or private investors performed work that supporters originally imagined as national. The result by 1848 was a much more connected national economy alongside an unresolved constitutional debate. Federal policy had helped create conditions for growth in some ways, but Americans continued to resist policies they interpreted as special privilege. Therefore both change and continuity characterized the era: expectations for economic development grew, while the political conflict over the legitimate reach of national economic authority remained remarkably durable.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the market revolution from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: Before 1800, most American families lived near farms and exchanged many goods in local markets. Roads were poor, travel was slow, and the different regions were not always connected. Population growth and settlement after independence created pressure for a larger economy. The market revolution came from many developments, and every development was important in its own way, so picking one main cause does not really explain it. Transportation changed during this period. The Erie Canal connected western farms to New York City, while the National Road and steamboats also made movement easier. These projects are examples of internal improvments. Railroads began to appear and eventually became even more important. New machines were another part of the story. Eli Whitney's cotton gin cleaned cotton faster. Textile mills gathered workers and machines in one building, including the women who worked at Lowell. There was also more immigration, which gave growing cities additional labor. Farmers could send crops farther away instead of only using them at home. Factories sold manufactured products, and banks provided money. Telegraph lines later allowed information to move quickly. Each invention or group added something to a changing market. The North made more manufactured goods, the South grew cotton, and western farmers produced food. People moved between these areas and bought things made by strangers. Workers increasingly earned wages instead of producing everything for their own family. In conclusion the market revolution had transportation, inventions, workers, farms, factories, and trade. All of them occured between 1800 and 1848 and together the economy became a market revolution.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in mass political participation among white men from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: At the start of the republic, political participation was formally celebrated but was usually limited by state property requirements and by the belief that independent property owners made the best voters. From 1800 to 1848 participation among white men changed substantially as property barriers fell and campaigns became popular events, but it remained continuous in excluding women and most nonwhite Americans and in being controlled by political elites. In the early part of the period elections still involved important leaders. The election of 1800 moved power peacefully from the Federalists to Thomas Jefferson's party. It is often called a revolution, although most ordinary men did not vote directly for every office. Later states rewrote constitutions and more white men could vote without owning land. During the Jackson period, candidates tried to look like common people. Jackson's campaign used parades, newspapers, slogans, and public parties, while his opponents also organized voters. The election of 1828 was the first election with universal voting for every American man because Jackson removed all state property restrictions himself. Political conventions eventually replaced the older caucus method of nomination. The Whigs and Democrats built local organizations, printed partisan papers, and held rallies. These were signs that voting was becoming part of mass culture rather than only a discussion among gentlemen. Participation did not mean equal control. Party leaders chose messages and handed out government jobs to loyal supporters. Enslaved men, free Black men in many states, Native Americans, and all women remained outside national voting. One dramatic change therefore existed beside a broad limitation. There were also arguments about whether popular politics improved democracy or simply made campaigning louder. Supporters said a larger electorate made officials responsive. Critics pointed to crowds and emotional appeals. By 1848 a white man without much property had far more opportunity to vote and join a party than his grandfather did, but officeholders were still generally wealthy and well connected. The era was more democratic for white males, not democratic for everybody.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing Indigenous responses to United States expansion in the Southeast and the Old Northwest from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: United States settlement after 1800 pressed against Native homelands in both the Southeast and the Old Northwest. The Louisiana Purchase, the growth of cotton, and demands for western farms increased pressure, while federal officials used treaties and military power to obtain land. Indigenous peoples in both regions resisted removal and also tried accommodation, but southeastern nations relied more on centralized governments, farming changes, and American courts, whereas peoples in the Old Northwest more often built intertribal military coalitions. In the Southeast, the Cherokee adopted a written constitution, a newspaper, and a syllabary created by Sequoyah. Leaders hoped these changes would demonstrate that the Cherokee were a permanent nation capable of dealing with the United States. They also went to court. In Cherokee Nation v. Georgia and Worcester v. Georgia, the Cherokee challenged Georgia's attempt to extend state authority onto their land. The Supreme Court recognized limits on Georgia, but President Jackson did not protect the Cherokee. The Indian Removal Act and the later Trail of Tears showed the failure of the legal strategy. Every southeastern tribe followed the Cherokee plan because they all believed courts were their best defense, although some people did resist by force. In the Old Northwest, Tecumseh and Tenskwatawa tried to unite separate Native peoples against land cessions. They argued that no single nation could sell shared territory. The confederacy confronted the United States, and Harrison's forces attacked Prophetstown at Tippecanoe. During the War of 1812, Tecumseh allied with Britain because British support offered weapons and a possible barrier against American expansion. His death weakened the coalition and American settlement increased. These regional accounts show different responses even though neither region used only one method. Southeastern adoption of selected American institutions did not mean surrendering sovereignty, and northwestern alliance with Britain was a practical response rather than simple loyalty to Britain. Ultimately both approaches faced the same disadvantage: the United States had a growing settler population and could ignore agreements after Native resistance was divided or defeated. As a result, adaptation and armed confederacy both delayed expansion but did not stop it.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in African American freedom from 1863 to 1877.
student_response: Before 1863, most African Americans were enslaved in the South, while free Black communities in northern cities still faced voting restrictions, segregation, and danger from the Fugitive Slave Law. The Civil War turned preservation of the Union into a war that also destroyed slavery. From emancipation through 1877, African American freedom changed greatly through the abolition of slavery, citizenship, and male suffrage, but economic dependence and white violence continued to restrict what those legal freedoms meant in daily life. The Emancipation Proclamation freed enslaved people in rebelling areas and allowed Black men to enlist in the Union army. Military service, including by the 54th Massachusetts, made Black citizenship harder to deny. The Thirteenth Amendment then ended slavery nationwide. These changes transformed a person from legal property into someone able to form a recognized family, move, negotiate work, and pursue education. The Freedmen's Bureau, created in 1863, assisted with schools, contracts, and relief. Political freedom expanded further. The Fourteenth Amendment defined national citizenship and equal protection, and the Fifteenth prohibited denying male suffrage because of race. African American voters helped elect Republican state governments, and Black officeholders such as Hiram Revels served in Congress. These were real shifts in federal power and public participation, not simply promises. Yet former slaveholders tried to preserve labor control. Black Codes limited movement and contracts after the war, and sharecropping trapped many families in crop-lien debt. The Ku Klux Klan used murder and intimidation to stop voting. Congress passed Enforcement Acts and federal troops sometimes protected elections, but protection was inconsistant and declined as northern commitment weakened. Freedom therefore had an important continuity: white southerners could no longer legally own Black people, but they still controlled much land, credit, and local courts. Reconstruction made the federal government a guarantor of rights in principle, although states often evaded it in practice. By the Compromise of 1876, troops were withdrawn and Redeemer governments returned. African Americans had constitutional rights that slavery had made impossible, and communities built churches, schools, and families with greater autonomy. However, without land redistribution or lasting federal enforcement, legal citizenship did not produce equal power. The period was revolutionary in law and politics but incomplete in economics and personal safety.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in the balance of federal and state power from 1844 to 1877.
student_response: Before 1844, fights over tariffs and nullification had already raised the question of whether states could resist national law. Expansion after the Mexican War made the issue more explosive because every new territory reopened the slavery argument. From 1844 to 1877 the balance changed decisively toward federal supremacy through Civil War and Reconstruction, yet states retained enough control over policing and elections to obstruct new national rights. A transformation, but an incomplete one. At first federal institutions protected state slave systems as well as restraining them. Dred Scott declared in 1857 that Congress could not prohibit slavery in territories. Then secession claimed the ultimate state power. Union victory rejected that claim, while wartime taxation, conscription, and emancipation greatly enlarged Washington's reach. The Thirteenth Amendment abolished slavery inside every state. The Fourteenth Amendment, ratified in 1866, made national citizenship and equal protection limits on states, and the Fifteenth restricted state control of voting. Congress enforced those principles with Reconstruction Acts placing the former Confederacy under military districts. A direct federal intervention in state government. The change had limits. President Johnson argued restoration belonged mainly to the executive and allowed southern states considerable freedom. Those governments passed Black Codes. Congress answered with federal legislation and impeachment, demonstrating that the branches also disagreed over how national power should operate. Even after amendments, local officials controlled juries and elections, and terror groups could destroy Black political participation unless federal troops intervened. This contradiction shows the new balance: constitutional authority shifted upward, but implementation still depended on action within states. By 1877 northern willingness to enforce Reconstruction collapsed and home rule returned across the South. States could not legally secede or restore slavery, so the antebellum balance was not restored. Still, federal retreat let state governments hollow out federally guaranteed freedom. National supremacy won on paper and battlefield; state power endured in practice.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of United States territorial expansion from 1844 to 1860.
student_response: The United States got bigger in the middle of the 1800s because people wanted it to be bigger and there was land nearby. Americans moved west in wagons and looked for places to live, and the government agreed that growth was a good thing, there were also other countries involved and sometimes they disagreed about the borders. Expansion happened for political reasons, economic reasons, and social reasons. Farmers needed farms, merchants wanted trade, and politicians wanted Americans to support them. Newspapers talked about the West and made it sound promising. Some people went because their families went, while others wanted a new beginning. The country also believed its system was successful, so spreading the country seemed natural to many citizens, but not everyone agreed and there were arguments. Different sections worried that new places could help another section more. The government made negotiations and wars and then territories became part of the nation, this took several years and caused more disagreement after it happened. Therefore there were many causes of territorial expansion from 1844 to 1860, and those causes explain why the map looked different by the end.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of the decline of Reconstruction governments from 1868 to 1877.
student_response: Reconstruction governments declined mainly because white southern resistance combined with declining northern willingness to keep spending political and military energy on racial equality, while economic worries drew public attention elsewhere. These forces made the governments easier to portray as illegitimate and harder to defend. Reconstruction governments were controversial because the country did not agree about what equality required. Some citizens believed any federal involvement in local voting was dictatorship. Others believed national protection was necessary wherever a majority threatened a minority. This disagreement continued for years and made every election feel temporary. Southern opponents used speeches, social pressure, and claims about local self-government to make cooperation look like betrayal. Northern voters gradually became less interested. They wanted political stability and worried about taxes, jobs, and charges that public officials were dishonest. Stories of corruption were treated as proof that the entire effort had failed, even though corruption can exist under any party. The debate was also influenced by later examples of civil rights conflict. In Brown v. Board of Education, the Supreme Court declared segregated schools unconstitutional, but states resisted and forced the national government to intervene. The Montgomery Bus Boycott showed how organized Black communities could challenge local rules when ordinary politics did not respond. Television coverage of violence in Birmingham finally convinced many northern viewers that federal civil rights laws were needed. These events reveal why Reconstruction governments lost support: Americans do not maintain attention to civil rights unless dramatic images force them to notice. Economic policy also changed priorities. During the New Deal the federal government created relief programs and regulations because unemployment had become more important than older sectional questions. Politicians learned that promises about wages and economic security attract more votes than arguments about constitutional rights. That same public tendency hurt Reconstruction governments once people became tired of conflict. There were also accusations that inexperienced voters and officials could not govern responsibly. Such accusations gave opponents a respectable sounding reason for recovering power without openly saying they opposed equality. Because government authority depends on people accepting election results, constant claims that an administration is corrupt can weaken it even when some of its policies are useful. Eventually the national government preferred reconciliation between white northerners and southerners. The governments then had fewer allies and could not survive resistance on their own. In this sense their decline was not caused by one bad law or leader. It came from organized hostility in the South and political exhaustion in the North, with economic distraction accelerating both.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing presidential and congressional approaches to Reconstruction from 1865 to 1877.
student_response: The Civil War ended slavery and defeated secession, but it did not give the country an agreed plan for returning southern states or protecting freedpeople. This situation created a struggle between the president and Congress. Presidential and congressional Reconstruction were different in some respects and similar in other respects, and both were plans for fixing the country after a war. Lincoln had proposed a Ten Percent Plan that allowed a state to return after ten percent of its voters took a loyalty oath. President Andrew Johnson continued a presidential style of quick restoration. He issued pardons and let southern states organize governments with relatively few requirements. These governments passed Black Codes. Johnson also fought with Congress, including by vetoing bills. Congress had its own approach. Republicans passed the Fourteenth Amendment and divided the South into military districts under the Reconstruction Acts. Congress wanted southern states to write new constitutions and protect Black male voting before full return. The Freedmen's Bureau was another program during Reconstruction and provided schools and help. Johnson supported all of these congressional measures at first but then Radical Republicans removed him from office for joining their party. The presidential section of Reconstruction was more forgiving. It concentrated on loyalty and restoring states. Congress added military oversight and civil rights, although members of Congress did not all agree with each other. Later, Franklin Roosevelt's New Deal helped Reconstruction by creating jobs for former slaves and rebuilding southern farms. This became a third approach based on economic recovery rather than punishment. There were problems with every approach. White southerners resisted, freedpeople needed land and safety, and northern voters did not want conflict forever. The Compromise of 1877 was one final presidential Reconstruction plan, since the president decided to compromise with Congress and remove all southern governors. The executive and legislative branches therefore each acted at different times. One was generally quicker and one had more conditions, but both claimed that their own policies would restore the Union.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing the effects of United States western expansion on Indigenous and Mexican American communities from 1848 to 1877.
student_response: The Mexican-American War transferred a huge western region to the United States, and the discovery of gold brought many more settlers into California. American expansion then placed older communities under a government dominated by newcomers. From 1848 to 1877, both Indigenous and Mexican American communities lost land and political control as United States settlers moved west, but Indigenous peoples were more often removed or confined as separate nations while Mexican Americans were formally treated as citizens and then pushed aside through courts, taxes, and violence. Mexican Americans living in the transferred territory were covered by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. It promised that they could become United States citizens and that their property would be respected. In practice, proving a land claim was expensive and took a long time. Many families sold land to pay lawyers or taxes. English-speaking settlers also became a majority in places where Spanish-speaking communities had lived before the border changed. This reduced Mexican American influence even though they were citizens. Indigenous communities were usually treated differently because the government considered them tribes rather than prior Mexican citizens. Settlers wanted mining areas, railroad paths, farms, and ranch land. Federal officials made reservations and tried to move Native groups onto them. When groups resisted, the Army fought western wars and destroyed villages or food supplies. The Navajo Long Walk is one example of a community being forced away from its homeland. Native children were also expected to give up their cultures in government schools, which expansion supporters described as civilization. Both groups experienced prejudice and violence. Mexican Americans were called foreigners even on land that had once been Mexico, while Native people were described as barriers to progress. The results were therefore alike in loss of property and local power. They were different in legal form: Mexican Americans could point to citizenship and treaty promises, whereas Indigenous nations faced reservation policy and military conquest. Neither protection worked very well when courts and officials sided with incoming settlers. Western expansion enlarged opportunities for Americans moving west, but it greatly narrowed the independence of the people already living there.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of organized agrarian protest from 1865 to 1896.
student_response: After the Civil War farming became more connected to national markets, while railroads and banks had power over how crops reached buyers and how farmers borrowed money. Organized agrarian protest grew because farmers faced falling crop prices, debt, expensive railroad shipping, and a money supply they believed was too small. The Grange first gave rural families a social organization and it also pushed states to regulate railroad rates, the Farmers' Alliances later built cooperatives so farmers could buy and sell together, and the Populist Party carried demands into elections. Farmers wanted the free coinage of silver because more money might raise prices and make debts easier to repay, they also supported government ownership or control of railroads when private rates seemed unfair. The crop-lien system kept many southern farmers borrowing against the next harvest, while plains farmers could lose income when overproduction lowered wheat prices. These problems encouraged people who lived far apart to meet, publish newspapers, and form third parties. The movement reached a peak when William Jennings Bryan defended silver in 1896, although he was technically a Democrat and not only a farm leader. Agrarian protest was therefore organized because individual farmers had little leverage against creditors and railroad companies. They joined groups to make their complaints larger, but disagreements over race, region, and silver made unity difficult.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of industrial consolidation in the United States from 1865 to 1898.
student_response: After the Civil War, railroads connected regional producers to a national market and a growing urban population purchased goods in much larger quantities. Industrial consolidation resulted mainly from the enormous capital and coordination required by this national economy, while harsh competition encouraged owners to absorb rivals and control prices. New technology allowed factories to produce steel, oil, and other goods on a scale that small firms could not easily match. But large plants and railroad networks were expensive. Investment bankers and corporations gathered money from many investors, allowing a company to expand beyond one town. Consolidation also reduced uncertainty. John D. Rockefeller used horizontal integration to bring competing oil refineries into Standard Oil, and the trust arrangement placed their stock under central control. With fewer competitors, Standard Oil could negotiate railroad rebates and influence prices. Andrew Carnegie used vertical integration by controlling mines, transportation, and steel mills. Each stage supplied the next, lowering costs and reducing dependence on outside firms. Government policy generally allowed this growth. Protective tariffs limited some foreign competition, courts often defended contracts and property, and early enforcement of the Sherman Antitrust Act was weak. Consolidation was not only the result of greedy personalities. A continental market rewarded companies that could standardize products and distribute them widely. At the same time, the drive to escape price wars made monopoly attractive. Thus technology and national transportation made giant firms possible, and competition plus permissive policy made consolidation a practical strategy from 1865 to 1898.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing the membership strategies and goals of the Knights of Labor and the American Federation of Labor from 1869 to 1898.
student_response: The Knights wanted many kinds of workers in one broad reform movement, while the AFL organized skilled workers into seperate craft unions and looked for immediate gains in wages, hours, and conditions. Both responded to the insecurity of industrial wage labor, but their memberships led them toward different goals and methods: the Knights tried to change the whole economic system through solidarity and reform, while the AFL used focused bargaining to win achievable benefits for workers with leverage. After the Civil War, factories, railroads, and corporations grew rapidly. Immigrants and rural Americans entered wage jobs where depressions, long hours, and dangerous conditions could quickly ruin a family. Earlier local craft groups were too small for this new national economy. The Knights of Labor, led by Terence Powderly, welcomed skilled and unskilled laborers, women, and Black workers, although it excluded some groups such as Chinese immigrants. It promoted producer cooperatives, the eight-hour day, and an end to child and convict labor. Its broad membership fit the idea that all producers shared an interest against concentrated wealth. The organization sometimes used strikes, but Powderly was uneasy about them and prefered education and political reform. After the Haymarket bombing, people connected broad labor radicalism with violence even though the Knights had not planned the bombing, membership fell as employers and newspapers attacked it. Samuel Gompers's American Federation of Labor followed a narrower strategy. The AFL was a federation of skilled craft unions, and these members were harder for employers to replace. It concentrated on bread and butter issues such as higher pay, shorter hours, and safer shops, using collective barganing and strikes rather than trying to replace capitalism with cooperatives. This excluded many unskilled workers, women, and African Americans in practice. The difference was not that the AFL had no larger values or the Knights never wanted wages. Both demanded the eight-hour day and both needed worker unity. Instead, their strategies reflected who belonged: a universal organization had ambitious social goals but many conflicting interests, while skilled craft workers could pressure a particular employer over a specific contract. The Knights were more inclusive for their time but more vulnerable during strikes and public backlash. The AFL sacrificed inclusivness for stable dues, bargaining power, and survival. That is why the Knights declined in the 1880s while the AFL became the more durable federation by 1898.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of United States overseas expansion from 1880 to 1898.
student_response: American overseas expansion involved a number of episodes and doctrines. The Panama Canal. The Roosevelt Corollary. Dollar Diplomacy. Missionary language and naval stations. Later entry into the First World War. Each item showing the United States becoming involved beyond the continent. President Theodore Roosevelt encouraged Panama to separate from Colombia and then gained the canal zone. A canal shortened naval travel between the oceans and also helped trade. The Roosevelt Corollary announced that the United States could intervene in Latin American countries when instability threatened debts. It expanded the older Monroe Doctrine into an international police power. In the Dominican Republic, American officials supervised customs revenue. Under President Taft, Dollar Diplomacy used loans and investment to influence other nations. Nicaragua became an example of financial and military involvement. Woodrow Wilson criticized that method but still sent forces into Haiti and the Dominican Republic. He also occupied Veracruz during turmoil in Mexico and sent Pershing after Pancho Villa. Not isolation. Strategic thinkers believed sea power required a modern navy, bases, and canals. Missionaries and business leaders added moral and commercial language. When Europe entered war, Wilson first declared neutrality. Submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram eventually contributed to American entry, and troops went to France. The Fourteen Points then described a new international role. The Senate did not accept the League of Nations, demonstrating continued reluctance. In the Pacific, the Open Door notes defended access to the Chinese market, and the Great White Fleet displayed naval strength. These examples are sometimes grouped together even though they happened under different presidents. Business, strategy, ideals, and presidential ambition were all present. Some interventions claimed to bring order while also protecting investors. Some claimed to defend self-government but put American troops in another country. During the 1920s, naval limitation conferences and arguments over war debts added another question about how active the nation should remain overseas after victory. More debates, with different answers. Consequently overseas policy cannot be explained as simply generous or simply selfish. It changed according to the location and president, with no one cause clearly more important than the rest.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing systems of racial control in the New South and the trans-Mississippi West from 1877 to 1898.
student_response: From 1877 to 1898, the New South and the trans-Mississippi West both used law, economic pressure, and violence to keep racial minorities below white settlers, but southern control sought a rigid separation of Black and white citizens while western control more often tried to remove minority landholding and culture. This made the systems alike in purpose but somewhat different in form. In the South, white leaders made rules that kept people apart in transportation, schools, jobs, and public spaces. They said this was traditional and fair even though the better resources always went to white people. Voting was also made harder through confusing requirements that local white officials could enforce however they wanted. If legal restrictions did not work, mobs and threats discouraged challenges. All southern white people supported this arrangement, and no Black residents could resist it. Western racial control dealt with several populations. White settlers believed the land should be used their way and treated communities already there as obstacles. Officials moved people, broke up communities, and pressured children to abandon their language. Mexican families were often considered foreigners regardless of how long they had lived in a place. Asian workers could be welcomed for hard labor and then blamed for low wages. Like the South, western towns sometimes separated schools or neighborhoods and allowed violence to go unpunished. The main difference was geography. Southern whites lived near a large Black population they wanted as labor, so they developed a permanent daily system of separation. Western settlers wanted land and cultural sameness, so control centered more on displacement and assimilation. Still, both systems let whites claim democracy while denying equal power to other races. The laws looked neutral to supporters, but they were applied by the group that already controlled courts, land, and weapons.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing the causes and consequences of the Great Migration and Dust Bowl migration from 1915 to 1940.
student_response: In the decades before 1915, the United States became more industrial and cities offered factory work, while Jim Crow laws and racial violence limited African American life in the South. Farming families in the Great Plains also depended on weather and crop markets. These conditions helped set up two large movements of people. The Great Migration and the Dust Bowl migration had some causes and results that were similar and some that were different, and both changed where Americans lived. During the Great Migration, African Americans left the rural South and moved to northern cities. Chicago was a major destination. Newspapers told people about jobs, and family members who had already moved could help later arrivals. Southern discrimination was another reason for leaving. Northern factories needed workers, especially when immigration slowed during World War I. Migrants built neighborhoods and brought music, churches, and food with them. Most were immediately accepted by white northern residents because racial prejudice only existed in the South. The Dust Bowl migration involved farm families leaving dry areas for California. Dust storms destroyed farms, and people had trouble paying debts. Many traveled west because advertisements promised agricultural jobs. California growers needed pickers, but migrants often found low wages and camps instead of the success they expected. They were usually called Okies whether or not they came from Oklahoma. Both migrations involved people looking for jobs and following relatives or information about a better place. Both also created crowded housing and competition for work at the destination. However one migration came from racial oppression and wartime industry, and the other came from bad weather and farm failure. The consequences were also different because African American migration created the Harlem Renaissance in every northern city, while Dust Bowl migrants mainly created new farms of their own in California. By World War II almost all southern African Americans had moved north and almost all Plains farmers had moved west. Government programs eventually helped both groups find houses and jobs. Popular songs and family stories remembered the trips, making each movement part of the identity of the communities that arrived in their new region. The two migrations demonstrate that Americans move when one region has hardship and another seems to have opportunity. There were pushes and pulls in both cases, but their exact details were not the same.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of Progressive reform from 1890 to 1920.
student_response: During the Gilded Age, rapid industrialization produced giant corporations and expanding cities, while political machines and weak regulation seemed unable to deal with new social problems. Progressive reform from 1890 to 1920 arose mainly because middle-class Americans feared the poverty, corruption, and loss of public control created by industrial capitalism, with journalists and organized activists turning those concerns into political pressure. Urban growth made poor housing, disease, and unsafe work more visible. Settlement houses such as Jane Addams's Hull House offered services to immigrant neighborhoods and gave educated reformers direct knowledge of city conditions. Muckrakers published investigations for a broad reading public. Upton Sinclair's The Jungle described the meatpacking industry, and Ida Tarbell wrote about Standard Oil. Reformers also challenged machines by supporting direct primaries, initiatives, referendums, and the direct election of senators. These measures were part of the Progressive era. Workers demanded compensation after accidents, shorter hours, and limits on child labor. Women participated in clubs and suffrage organizations because reform offered a public role before they had national voting rights. Not all Progressives agreed. Some wanted experts to make efficient government, others wanted voters to have more direct power, and some used reform language to restrict immigrants. The New Deal was the most important Progressive program because Franklin Roosevelt responded to the 1907 depression with Social Security, bank insurance, and the WPA. These national programs proved that earlier local charity was not enough. Reformers held conferences, wrote reports, and asked city officials to collect information before acting. Their faith in investigation became another recognizable feature. Progressivism also came from the Social Gospel, which applied Christian responsibility to poverty, and from farmers who had criticized railroads and concentrated wealth. Presidents began to respond as public opinion changed. Overall the problems of cities and corporations created the demand, while writers and associations gave that demand direction. The result was reform at city, state, and federal levels, although discrimination and unequal power remained.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in federal restrictions on civil liberties during wartime from 1917 to 1945.
student_response: Civil liberties had already been contested in earlier crises, especially when the Civil War government suspended habeas corpus to suppress threats to the Union. From 1917 to 1945, federal restriction remained continuous in targeting people portrayed as disloyal during national emergency, but it changed from prosecuting antiwar speech in World War I to mass confinement based heavily on ancestry in World War II. Wartime fear repeatedly outweighed individual rights. Espionage Act of 1917. Sedition Act of 1798. Federal officials used these laws against people who interfered with the draft or criticized the war. Socialist leader Eugene V. Debs went to prison after an antiwar speech, and the government also raided radical organizations. In Schenck v. United States, the Supreme Court upheld punishment for distributing anti-draft leaflets and introduced the clear and present danger idea. The restrictions directly narrowed speech that would normally be political debate. After the war, the Red Scare and Palmer Raids extended the atmosphere against radicals and immigrants. World War II produced a different method. Executive Order 9066 authorized removal from military areas, and over one hundred thousand Japanese Americans, most of them citizens, were sent to camps. Their homes and businesses were lost or sold while no individualized proof of disloyalty was required. Korematsu v. United States upheld the exclusion. Germans and Italians were never viewed with any suspicion, showing that the policy was entirely unique to Japanese ancestry. There was also continuity in official justification. In both wars, leaders claimed extraordinary action was temporary and necessary for security. Courts generally deferred to that claim instead of applying peacetime protection. Yet the object changed: World War I enforcement centered on speech, political organizations, and opposition to conscription, whereas the Japanese policy treated ancestry and residence as evidence. Some dissent existed. Justice Holmes later defended a freer marketplace of ideas, and Justice Murphy condemned racism in the internment cases. Still, these objections did not prevent punishment during the emergency itself. The First Amendment was never formally abolished and elections continued, but particular groups bore restrictions that the majority did not. By 1945 the tools had changed from criminal trials for words to administrative mass removal, while the pattern of defining unpopular minorities as security dangers persisted.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining how wartime mobilization caused social and economic change in the United States from 1941 to 1945.
student_response: The Great Depression left millions unemployed and expanded federal economic action through the New Deal, but full recovery had not arrived before war began in Europe. After Pearl Harbor brought the United States into World War II, mobilization caused rapid economic and social change by directing industry toward military production, pulling women and minorities into new jobs, and moving millions of Americans to defense centers, although discrimination continued. The War Production Board told companies to shift from consumer goods to planes, ships, tanks, and weapons. Factories ran many shifts, unemployment fell, and rationing limited items such as gasoline and meat so resources could go to the military. War bonds and higher taxes helped pay for production. This demand ended the Depression more completely than New Deal relief had. Labor shortages changed who worked. Women represented by Rosie the Riveter entered industrial jobs that had been treated as men's work. African Americans moved from the South to western and northern defense cities in another Great Migration. A threatened march by A. Philip Randolph pushed Roosevelt to ban discrimination by defense contractors, even though segregation and workplace prejudice remained. Mexican workers entered through the bracero program to fill agricultural labor needs. These shifts caused conflict as well as opportunity. Crowded boomtowns faced housing shortages, and racial tension produced riots. Japanese Americans on the West Coast were sent to internment camps, showing that wartime unity did not mean equal rights. The GI Bill gave returning soldiers college tuition during the war and immediately made every veteran middle class, though African American soldiers received exactly the same access everywhere. Mobilization therefore changed more than the amount of production. Federal planning tied business to Washington, wages and employment grew, and groups excluded from some occupations gained experience and expectations they would use after 1945. Still, many women were expected to leave factory work when men returned, and racial barriers survived. The war accelerated economic recovery and migration without eliminating inequality.
